definition: Describing conditioned phenomena as unstable, changing, and not lasting.
notes: The project treats this entry as a major doctrinal term because it functions within the three marks framework and in close relation to dissatisfaction and not-self. The house preference is the adjective impermanent in direct translation because it keeps the term tied to how phenomena are being described rather than turning it immediately into an abstraction. Impermanence remains appropriate when prose needs to name the characteristic itself. The OSF glossary stresses the term as the constant and ever-changing nature of phenomena; the repository records that as compatible explanatory emphasis. SN 22.59 also supports keeping the family linkage from impermanence to dissatisfaction explicit.

context_rules.context: In direct doctrinal statements describing phenomena
context_rules.notes: Use this as the default adjectival rendering.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose naming the characteristic itself
context_rules.notes: Use when English needs the abstract noun rather than the adjective.

context_rules.context: In passages emphasizing instability or unreliable changeability
context_rules.notes: Use this as a controlled alternate when impermanent sounds too static.

example_phrases.notes: Classic three-marks formulation using the adjectival sense.
Shows the doctrinal linkage from impermanence to dissatisfaction.

authority_basis.scope: Named in notes as support for the current anicca policy.
Supports the progression from impermanence to dissatisfaction and keeps the term tied to the governed three-marks family.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'impermanent' as the default rendering in the entry's governed translation contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Yield to recorded context_rules, source-facing technical use, or other explicitly documented local reasons rather than rotating among alternates ad hoc.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents drift back toward 'temporary' or other unrecorded alternates.
